All right, I'm David Pinsoff, I'm David Pietraszewski. This is Evolutionary Psychology, the podcast, which is a podcast about actual evolutionary psychology by actual evolutionary psychologists. Enjoy.
David, today we have somebody who needs no introduction, Lita Cosmedes. Yes, Lita Kosmedes, together with John Toobie, invented the field of evolutionary psychology, which is the name of this podcast, in fact, in case you didn't know that. Yeah.
so it was a real treat to talk to late out we kind of wanted to save her for a while we're almost a year now I think since we started Almost a year, yeah. I was going to count the number of guests, but yeah, I think we're getting up on a year, yeah. but we wanted to like get a lot of practice before we we invited late on to like you know
Figure out how to do this podcasting thing. That's right. We wanted to go from JV to varsity or at least from like kindergarten to elementary school, although we're probably just not even we're just at the crawling to cruising stage here.
Yeah, but anyway, I mean, like, it was just a fantastic conversation, just a total delight talking to Leda and just learning like a really interesting history and sociology of science and like how this thing got off the ground and there are a lot of fun human stories that emerged. And yeah, it was a very reflective episode about how evolutionary psychology got going, where it's headed, whether there.
Are there any regrets? We talked a bit about economics and rationality and stuff, but mainly it was just kind of a sweeping retrospective of how we got here. Yeah, it's an important part of the scientific record. And I think, you know, when you're talking about at the level of Lita or John, we're just sort of part of the messengers of getting this in the scientific record. And I'm happy that we got this a little bit and I'm sure we'll have.
I'll have Lida back on. But yeah, without further ado, here is sort of episode one of Lida on evolutionary psychology of the podcast. Enjoy.
Lida, it is well overdue for us to have you on a podcast about evolutionary psychology. I actually on purpose wanted to not have you on right away, though, because I also we also wanted to have people who are sort of younger doing the work in their earlier career stages on many of them. We asked how they got into it.
went into psych or into evolutionary approaches to behavior and
almost invariably somebody read a book or a paper that inspired them probably I don't know David you tell me but like I think 30 to 75 percent of those was a paper or book by you and John yeah paper or even a conversation or a class exactly they sat in on a class or whatever you know yeah yes except for Ed Hagan who apparently did not like John's class and disagreed with John while he was sitting in the class which to me was hilarious
hilarious like i i would have loved to talk to ed hagen back then um uh but uh but but this is all lead into um your story about how you got into evolutionary psychology cannot be i read myself and therefore i was inspired to do it um so so this this begs the question of uh how does a a younger
or very young Lita Cosmedes, you know, start moving towards taking an evolutionary approach to psychology. And that's obviously a big, a big question. And we don't Not have to do really. the whole, the whole, it's not okay, then if we please.
If I remember correctly, you're one of the people that you found the adapted mind in your college library, which Right. makes the pain of putting it together worth it. Right. Exactly. It was it was human universals and then human universals led me to the index of human universals and then it was to a bunch of your papers and then the adapted mind. Yeah.
Um, well, okay, well, this will sound odd, but I was a child during the late 60s, and everybody, everything seemed, everybody seemed to be angry at one another, and I lived in a suburb of Washington, D.C., and there were...
protest that was the daily news which huge protest was happening and it was during the Vietnam era and just and then there was the youth culture and the generation gap and everybody just seemed angry all the time and I kept thinking there has to be a better way for people to live
And then I kept thinking about that through junior high and high school, especially high school, and thinking about that, I thought, yeah, but how would you know what that was unless you knew something about human nature? So I thought, OK, well, then I got to I need to understand human nature. And and it was sort of in a vague sense. I mean, I read and.
10th and 11th grade I read and then reread B.F. Skinner's Walden 2, which is his, it was a very famous book that almost nobody knows anymore. It's his utopian novel in which a behaviorist, basically him, is running
society and running a community. Walden is, it's as a Walden pond, but this is Walden too. And he's running it based on behaviorist principles and positive and negative reinforcement. And it was very clear that he thought that the mind was a blank slate, that everything people do is because of positive and negative reinforcement.
which is sometimes called the equal potentiality assumption that any stimulus has an equal potential for reinforcing any response and I thought that was just wrong I thought it was really wrong it made no sense to me because
life is too various so sitting in a boring suburb of Washington DC you know I thought well if what he's saying was true you'd see people like fleeing from the chandeliers I mean you wouldn't everybody was basically behaving the same way you know
And I just thought you'd see a hugely more variation between people if everything that you did was because of a history of reinforcement. And also, like, how consistent can that history of reinforcement be? It made no sense to me. Plus, it was, you know, he was basically arguing things like.
you know that you could through the right history of reinforcement you could make you know a mother just as happy to be nursing somebody else's child as her own child or that you could make somebody deliriously happy to walk in on their spouse having sex with somebody else and and just with the right history of reinforcement they'd be thrilled by that and that just didn't seem that didn't make sense to me either
So when I, I mean, it always, my dad was a psychopharmacologist, a neuropsychopharmacologist, and used to pound the table about the importance of interdisciplinary research, which as a child, I always thought.
obviously why why is he why why is he pounding the table sometimes it would be about the Freudians because they were very much against psychopharmacology and but it was he was at NIH and he was always pounding him and I was like why do you have to pound the table I don't understand this table pounding about this because this is obvious and you know when I grew up I realized it was not obvious to most people and I guess also in that environment
the idea of taking a more of some sort of biological approach to understanding human nature made sense to me but at the time by the time I was a senior in college and ready to start college the the only thing like that that I knew about was was psychobiology but in my freshman year I realized I don't like psychobiology I mean I'm glad people do it
I'm glad people study neurons and hormones, but it wasn't telling me anything even remotely like what I wanted to know. It was not what I wanted to know. And by the end of my freshman year, I was having kind of a crisis because I didn't know what to major in. I hated the process. Like I would have gone nuts.
doing that research I'm glad there's people that do that research to be clear but it shouldn't be me and I thought how can I do I still want to understand human nature but how can I do that if I don't feel like I'm getting any answers and if the process is not something is something that I hate you know and
So that summer, I did a lot of things that summer. Among the things that I read, just because they were on my parents' shelf, I read Conrad Lorenz's On Aggression and Robert Ardrey's Territorial Imperative. And this was basically ethology. And if you think about it, ethology kind of is cognitive psychology, but with the full trappings of...
of an information processing revolution that happened in the next few years but it is it is trying to understand the design of the mind of non -human organisms in their natural environment and I thought that was very interesting I wasn't aware of the things about group selection versus non-group selection and in Conrad Lorenz's on aggression and so on but but I thought those were interesting but I still had no idea what I had to declare a major
And so when I got back to school like a week earlier or something, and so I thought freshman week they had.
the year before a year before they had they had told us to get to these two books to read before we got there because freshman week there were going to be and there's going to be these two different lectures for us and one of them was E .O. Wilson and the book was The Ant Society and I thought that lecture was really interesting I thought I thought it was really interesting so not knowing what to do I thought
I'll talk to Professor Wilson about this. He seemed like an interesting guy. You know, I'll see what he says about this. And so I went to, I made an appointment with him and he was important for the rest of my life five minutes late.
And that was important because on the corkboard next to his door, there was a fly leaf of his new book that had just come out. This is fall of 1976. His new book, Sociobiology, which I'd never heard of before. And on the fly leaf, on the back fly leaf, it said this field is, it's not about.
how organisms do things it's about why they do things you know why do they behave this way rather than that way why why and and it's said to apply to humans too and I thought why that's what I'm that's what I want to know why why do we behave the way that we do so
with the hubris of a 19 year old when he he showed up I said okay professor Wilson the sociobiology thing that you wrote that's what I want to major in that's what I want to do I want to understand human nature I wanted to do it from this perspective what should I do what should I major in
And he said very sensibly, he said, well, you could either major in psychology or in biology. He said, if you later decide to go to graduate school in evolutionary biology, that would be hard to do if you majored in psychology as an undergraduate. On the other hand, if you've got a good foundation in evolutionary biology, then you would probably still be able to go to graduate school in psychology if you wanted. So I said, OK, I'll do that.
um and and then he said and then uh well this this had good and bad consequences he says then um you should get this uh younger professor Robert Rivers to be your advisor and so I said okay and uh so so that that's how well but see the thing I still wanted to understand human nature and that's more than
the questions about ultimate causation that's more than why it's it's how but it's how on a cognitive level and and and cognitive thinking was it was on the newer side at that time and um but um
It was clear that that was necessary, that that was a necessary level of explanation, and what information are you processing? What's the design of the mind? And that wasn't what was happening in, so there was a very, there was, there was Irvin Bohr, who's an anthropologist.
who was part of several different revolutions, was at Harvard and he had something called the Simeon Seminar. And the Simeon Seminar met like four or five times a semester and it was in Herb DeBoer's living room.
And some of those famous people in the field came through and talked at that, including John Maynard Smith, a great evolutionary biologist.
Richard Dawkins did, W.D. Hamilton, Irv had a lot of graduate students who were primatologists and doing field work in primatology and so it was like his lab group in a way, but with the people coming through just astonishing. Joseph Sheffer who did the red light of incest, who did the blood studies on incest, all these people were coming through Irv's living room.
And that was phenomenal and a really good background in evolutionary biology and some thinking by people who are approaching behavior from an evolutionary point of view, but very broadly. So a lot of what they were doing was noting correspondences that were kind of loose between selection pressures and social organization.
So, yes, kin selection implies that kinship will be a very important part of.
of human life and who we help and so on and yes anthropologically you see that many societies are organized along kin lines although in complicated ways but it was too it was too loose of a correspondence it was it was really it was there was a missing level there was selection pressures and then there was manifest social organization
But the thing that's connecting them is missing because there has to be a mind that people have to have minds that are making decisions and wanting to do some things and not other things. I mean, that's the human nature part of it. And so in those in the seminars that that was largely missing and and people would also engage in fitness teleology.
including by the way trimmers they So say what is... so Yeah, go ahead. Yeah, sorry. fitness teleology is it's it's sort of assuming it's assuming that people are doing things to spread their genes as if they have a motive to spread their genes as if it's as if it's a depth psychology and that whatever you may be thinking on the surface deep down you have you want to spread your genes and and
without people really exactly understanding that there were psychological implications to what they were saying and so they'd say so they'd say things like well these monks medieval monks who are being celibate how are they they must be doing that to increase their inclusive fitness how is that how is that increasing their inclusive fitness oh well maybe they're helping their their siblings you know maybe it's a helpers at the nest kind of phenomenon
No, you have a psychology, it's leading you in different environments to do certain things. And it's not because deep down you're trying to spread your genes. I don't think it makes any sense to say that a celibate monk is choosing to be a celibate monk instead of having a family to spread his genes. That's not what natural selection is about. Natural selection is a, it's...
It's an account. It's a causal account of how you come to have the phenotype that you have. And in this case, it's a cognitive phenotype. In this case, it's how do you come to have the psychological mechanisms that you have? Okay. And so when a mother loves her child and is taking care of her child, it's not because deep down she wants to spread her genes. That has nothing to do with it. It's that.
mammal mothers who had psychological mechanisms that caused them to care for their children to provision their children to make sure they're safe had more children they had more offspring than those that neglected their offspring and that's why selection favored maternal psychologies that cause you to take care of your children it's not a depth psychology and so
A lot of that's what I mean by fitness teleology. People Hmm. are acting as if you have a deep down motive to spread your genes. And, and that's a forward looking thing. That's, that's, it's perspective. It's, it's, it's assuming that here I am. There are various things I could do right now. And I will choose them based on which one is going to spread my genes the most. And also natural selection is not about spreading your genes in general.
It's about why one genes underline one design feature outcompete those underlying another design feature. So this whole level of design was missing in how people were talking about it, except, of course, from George Williams and adaptation and natural selection, which many of us had read.
